# Questions in Livestream 21

Live Stream #21: The Weight of the Soul

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:21</a> | Is Israel mentioned on the Berlin pedestal relief in the Berlin Museum? | He said the proposed reading is highly controversial and likely misdated; he does not accept it as secure 18th-dynasty evidence for Israel. | He treats the Berlin pedestal relief claim as too uncertain to carry much historical weight. The proposed reading of Israel depends on disputed reconstruction and dating, so he would not use it as firm evidence for Israel in Egypt or Canaan before the better-attested Merneptah material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=783" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:03</a> | Did the ancient Egyptians emphasize public sports or games like the Greeks or Romans? | Not gladiatorial games. He pointed instead to wrestling, martial athletics, and board games such as senet. | He distinguishes Egyptian recreation from later Greco-Roman spectacle. Egypt had athletic and martial activities such as wrestling, along with games such as senet, but he does not describe anything like Roman gladiatorial entertainment as a major Egyptian institution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:14</a> | Why is there not a clearer intertextual polemic between Egyptian legged-serpent imagery and the serpent in Eden? | He said legged snakes were minor demonic figures in Egypt, while the stronger biblical parallel is the later seraph/winged-cobra imagery rather than Eden directly. | He says the Egyptian legged-serpent material is not prominent enough to explain the Eden serpent directly. The more useful comparison is later biblical seraph imagery, where winged fiery serpent symbolism overlaps more clearly with Egyptian cobra iconography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=1109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:29</a> | What does he think of the volcanic-Mount-Sinai theory? | He rejected it. He said there is no suitable Late Bronze eruption and most proposed "volcanoes" are not real volcanic candidates. | He rejects volcanic-Sinai proposals because the geography and chronology do not supply a plausible Late Bronze Age volcanic event. In his view, the common candidates either are not actual volcanoes or do not fit the period needed for the Exodus setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=1269" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:09</a> | Is there a connection between the Egyptian uraeus and the serpent on Moses's staff in Numbers 21, and how does that relate to John 3:14? | Yes, he saw real symbolic overlap. He said the serpent image is reused in scripture and ultimately redirected toward Christ being lifted up to bring healing. | He sees the bronze-serpent episode as part of a larger biblical reuse of serpent imagery, including Egyptian royal and protective associations. The symbol is not left as pagan iconography; John 3 redirects the lifted serpent motif toward Christ being lifted up as the source of healing and life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=1671" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:51</a> | Why does Isaiah 6:2 say the seraphim cover their feet? | He said the imagery reflects later symbolic development and signals modesty and reverence in God's presence, not a simple carryover from earlier iconography. | He treats Isaiah's seraphim as developed theological imagery rather than a flat borrowing from earlier serpent icons. Covering the feet functions as a sign of modesty and reverent self-concealment before God, fitting the holiness scene in Isaiah 6. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=1997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:17</a> | Is there anything in Christ's teaching that relates to Egyptian culture that most Christians miss? | He answered through Passover: Christ's work is framed as a second Passover and second Exodus, with the Egyptian background still structuring the symbolism. | He points to Passover and Exodus typology rather than a hidden Egyptian saying of Jesus. Christ's death and deliverance language are presented as a second Passover and second Exodus, so the Egyptian background remains central to Christian interpretation of redemption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=2264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:44</a> | What was it like to complete a master's degree while working full time? | He said it was difficult but doable with reduced course loads, night and early-morning classes, and a supportive employer. | He describes the process as demanding because full-time work limits the pace of graduate study. His practical answer is that it can be done by taking fewer courses, using unusual class times, and having an employer willing to accommodate the schedule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=2452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:52</a> | Should most of the Old Testament be considered monolatristic, and does Michael Heiser's view hold up? | He said monolatry is a useful category in part, but Heiser presses the model too far. | He allows that monolatry can describe some biblical and ancient Near Eastern language, especially where other spiritual beings are acknowledged. However, he thinks Heiser's framework can overextend the category and blur distinctions the biblical texts themselves maintain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=2693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:53</a> | Did the Persians conquer Egypt by using cats in battle? | He doubted the popular version of the story and did not think literal battle-cats explain the conquest. | He treats the cat story as a later or exaggerated account rather than a serious military explanation. Egyptian reverence for cats may have made the story memorable, but he does not think the Persian conquest turns on soldiers deploying cats as battlefield weapons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=2900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:20</a> | Do we have good archaeological evidence for the united monarchy, and is Iron Age downdating important? | Yes in a broad sense. He said the transition into early Iron Age culture matters, even if the full extent of the kingdom remains debated. | He says there is meaningful archaeological evidence for the period, but not in a way that settles every maximalist claim about the united monarchy. The dating of early Iron Age material remains important because it affects how one correlates settlement, state formation, and biblical chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=3169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:49</a> | What is going on in Genesis when Eliezer puts his hand under Abraham's thigh while making an oath? | He initially asked for a biblical reference, then explained it from Genesis 24:2 as a serious physical oath gesture, comparable to swearing with a hand over the heart or on a Bible. | After identifying the passage as Genesis 24, he explains the gesture as an embodied oath formula. He compares it to later symbolic oath gestures, stressing that the action marks the seriousness of the promise rather than functioning as a casual custom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=3229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:49</a> | If ancient peoples did not fully understand years early on, could that help explain the long ages in Genesis? | Possibly in Mesopotamian king lists, but he said Genesis is better read as talking about clan spans rather than literal individual lifespans. | He is open to chronological complications in some ancient lists, especially Mesopotamian royal traditions. For Genesis, though, he favors reading the long ages through clan or dynastic continuity, where named figures represent extended family lines rather than only individual biological lifespans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=3444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:24</a> | What is his view on repatriating artifacts such as the bust of Nefertiti? | He treated it as a mixed case: post-UNESCO illicit exports should go back, while older removals need case-by-case judgment. | He does not give a single rule for every artifact. Recent illicit removals after modern heritage agreements should be returned, but older museum holdings require attention to legal context, preservation, access, and the circumstances of acquisition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=4021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:01</a> | Are the sons of Noah meant to represent tribes? | Yes. He said the passage fits the larger clan and tribe structure that he sees throughout Genesis. | He reads the sons of Noah within Genesis's larger interest in peoples, lineages, and territories. The figures function as ancestral representatives for later groups, so the passage is about the mapping of clans and tribes, not just three isolated individuals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=4357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:37</a> | How much do language barriers affect scholars interacting with one another? | Less than people expect. He said serious scholars usually work across several research languages, especially English, French, and German. | He says language barriers matter, but trained scholars in these fields normally learn to read across major research languages. English, French, and German are especially important, so scholarship is not sealed off into one-language communities as much as outsiders may assume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=4612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:52</a> | Could he explain Leviticus 14:49-53? | He said it is a house-cleansing ritual for contamination, likely tied to mold and ritual purification rather than arbitrary magic. | He frames Leviticus 14 as a purification procedure for a contaminated house. The birds, blood, water, and release rite belong to the logic of ritual cleansing, likely responding to mold or similar household impurity rather than being random magical symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=5041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:01</a> | What does he think about evolution and the idea of an original African language in relation to Genesis? | He said he was still working through both issues, criticizing both evolution and American young-earth creationism while withholding judgment on the language claim. | He avoids a settled answer and presents both topics as issues he is still evaluating. He criticizes aspects of evolutionary claims and also American young-earth creationist assumptions, while treating the original-language proposal as something that would require more careful evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=5217" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:57</a> | Where does Behemoth in Job 40 come from? | He said there is no clear known parallel like there is for Leviathan, but Behemoth likely reflects regional mythic exchange around Job's Iron Age and Aramean setting. | He contrasts Behemoth with Leviathan, for which there are clearer mythic parallels. Behemoth may reflect an Iron Age environment of shared West Semitic and Aramean imagery, but he does not claim a specific source text or exact mythological equivalent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=5402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:02</a> | Has Kara Cooney's ideological perspective started to affect her academic work? | He said her early work was excellent, but he thinks later popular and academic work has become more ideologically shaped. | He separates his evaluation of Cooney's earlier scholarship from his concerns about later work. He praises the quality of the early academic material, while saying her more recent writing and public interpretation appear more influenced by ideological framing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=5635" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:55</a> | Did Zoroastrianism or Vedic religions influence early post-exilic Judaism, such as parallels between Isaiah 44 and Yasna 44? | He thought some Zoroastrian influence was plausible, while Vedic influence was doubtful; the extent of Zoroastrian influence remains debated. | He considers Persian-period Zoroastrian influence possible because of historical contact, though he does not treat every proposed parallel as decisive. Vedic influence is much less likely in his view, and the actual degree of Zoroastrian impact on Judaism remains a debated question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=5814" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:54</a> | What are the beasts in the book of Revelation? | He said Revelation's beast imagery is deeply rooted in the Old Testament, and many figures should be read through that context rather than as simple evil monsters. | He warns against reading Revelation's beasts as isolated fantasy creatures. The imagery draws heavily on Old Testament prophetic and apocalyptic backgrounds, so interpretation should start with those earlier biblical patterns before modern identifications are proposed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=6042" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:42</a> | Is Sarah Parcak's LiDAR work important or overblown, and has it led to major digs or finds? | He said her work is important, not overblown, and has produced results by revealing structures under the sand, though it will not solve everything. | He defends remote-sensing work as genuinely useful because it can reveal buried structures and guide archaeological investigation. At the same time, he treats it as one tool among others, not a method that eliminates the need for excavation or solves every question by itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vNKg8-e68Aw?t=6432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:12</a> | What is his favorite Egyptian animal or reptile from the Middle Kingdom era? | He did not choose one favorite; he mentioned the range of Egyptian fauna, liked snakes iconographically, and said he enjoyed seeing geckos but would not want to encounter snakes personally. | He answers lightly rather than naming a formal favorite. He notes the rich range of Egyptian animal imagery, says snakes are interesting iconographically, and contrasts that with personally enjoying harmless encounters such as geckos more than actual snakes. |
